resources.reason: Account resources model Cloudflare account container lifecycle rather than a zone or account setting.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider can import an account by account_id, but the resource owns account identity fields such as name, type, unit, and settings. Broad import would make Terraformer claim the account container itself instead of the settings lane tracked in issue #335.

resources.reason: Account DNS defaults remain deferred because the provider has no import path and API reads return effective defaults without explicit ownership markers.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 documents that cloudflare_account_dns_settings does not support terraform import. Its read path returns the full account DNS defaults object, including zone_defaults, but the response does not distinguish inherited/default values from settings explicitly owned by the account.

resources.reason: Billing subscription state is Cloudflare/account commerce-managed and should not be blindly emitted as Terraform-owned configuration.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource requires Billing Read/Write permissions and models rate_plan, renewal frequency, price, state, current billing periods, and externally_managed rate-plan metadata. Broad discovery would turn Cloudflare billing lifecycle state into generated infrastructure configuration.

resources.reason: Account-scoped API tokens expose a sensitive token value that is only available when the token is created.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider supports import by account_id/token_id, but the schema includes value as a Sensitive token string plus mutable issued/last-used status fields. Terraformer cannot recover the token value needed to reconstruct user-authored token configuration safely.

resources.reason: AI Search tokens require Cloudflare API credential material that broad import cannot safely reconstruct.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 marks cf_api_key as Sensitive and no_refresh, and import verification ignores cf_api_key. Terraformer cannot discover or emit a valid token resource without the required API key value.

resources.reason: API Shield discovery operation resources are review workflow state and the Terraform provider does not support import.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_api_shield_discovery_operation as not supporting terraform import. The resource marks discovered operations as review or ignored rather than owning durable endpoint configuration.

resources.reason: Deprecated API Shield operation schema-validation settings should not be added as new Terraformer coverage ahead of the replacement resource's import contract.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 marks cloudflare_api_shield_operation_schema_validation_settings as deprecated and directs users to cloudflare_schema_validation_operation_settings, whose provider docs state that terraform import is not currently supported.

resources.reason: Deprecated API Shield schemas require uploaded schema source and the Terraform provider does not expose a current import contract for this legacy resource.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 marks cloudflare_api_shield_schema as deprecated in favor of cloudflare_schema_validation_schemas and does not implement ResourceWithImportState for the legacy resource. Terraformer imports the replacement schema_validation_schemas resource instead.

resources.reason: Deprecated API Shield schema-validation settings should not be added as new Terraformer coverage ahead of the replacement resource's import contract.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 marks cloudflare_api_shield_schema_validation_settings as deprecated and directs users to cloudflare_schema_validation_settings, whose provider docs state that terraform import is not currently supported.

resources.reason: API token resources require token secret material that broad import cannot reconstruct.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider can import by token_id, but the resource exposes value as Sensitive token material and reads issued, modified, last-used, and status fields. Importing discovered tokens would emit incomplete credential lifecycle configuration.

resources.reason: Hostname-level Authenticated Origin Pull associations mix durable enablement with certificate lifecycle and private-key ownership.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider imports by zone_id/hostname and its nested certificate object includes private_key as Sensitive material. Terraformer now imports the zone-wide settings singleton only when enabled, while hostname certificate and association ownership needs a separate certificate-safe design.

resources.reason: Authenticated Origin Pull certificates require private key material that Cloudflare APIs cannot safely return.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider schema requires certificate, private_key, and zone_id. The private_key field is Sensitive private material, so Terraformer cannot reconstruct valid HCL from a discovered certificate ID alone.

resources.reason: Hostname Authenticated Origin Pull certificates require private key material that Cloudflare APIs cannot safely return.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider imports by zone_id/certificate_id, but the resource schema requires certificate and private_key, with private_key marked Sensitive. Terraformer cannot reconstruct valid HCL from a discovered hostname certificate ID alone.

resources.reason: Bot Management is a plan-gated zone singleton with many optional/default fields that need a dedicated explicit-ownership design.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 supports zone_id import, but the resource owns Bot Fight Mode, Super Bot Fight Mode, AI bot, content bot, robots.txt, and model-update settings whose availability and API response shape vary by zone plan. The provider preserves existing state for fields missing from API responses, so broad import can confuse defaults, stale zone configuration, and explicit user intent.

resources.reason: BYOIP prefixes are onboarding and validation lifecycle resources rather than safe broad-import inventory.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 requires ASN and CIDR and optionally an LOA document or delegated LOA creation, while read state exposes approval, ownership, IRR, RPKI validation states, and an ownership validation token. Broad import would turn prefix onboarding and validation workflow state into Terraform-owned configuration.

resources.reason: Cloudforce One requests represent submitted analysis work rather than stable infrastructure inventory.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource creates or tracks a request with content, priority, request_type, summary, and TLP while exposing mutable request status, token counts, and completion timestamps as read-only data.

resources.reason: Request messages are action-style updates to an existing Cloudforce One request.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource requires a request_id and optional message content, then reads author, message ID, creation time, and follow-on request status. Importing every message would reproduce request history instead of durable configuration.

resources.reason: Content Scanning status is a zone singleton setting whose Terraform provider resource does not support import.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_content_scanning as not supporting terraform import. The resource has a no-op delete path and toggles zone-level status, so broad import needs an explicit settings/default ownership design before Terraformer emits it.

resources.reason: Content Scanning expression resources do not expose a provider import/read path.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_content_scanning_expression as not supporting terraform import, and the generated resource has an empty Read method while owning payload expressions through a create/delete API.

resources.reason: Custom SSL certificate resources require private key material that Cloudflare APIs cannot safely return.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider imports by zone_id/custom_certificate_id, but creation requires certificate and private_key material. Terraformer cannot reconstruct the required private key for generated configuration.

resources.reason: Secondary DNS TSIG resources require a sensitive shared secret that Cloudflare APIs cannot safely return.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider imports by account_id/tsig_id, but the resource schema requires secret as a Sensitive field. Terraformer imports ACLs, peers, and incoming/outgoing transfer settings in this lane while leaving TSIG secret ownership out of broad discovery.

resources.reason: Hyperdrive configs require write-only origin credential material.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 requires origin.password and documents it as Sensitive with the API never returning this write-only value. The provider read path can only preserve write-only origin.password and origin.access_client_secret from prior state, which broad Terraformer discovery does not have.

resources.reason: Standalone list-item import would duplicate Terraformer's existing inline cloudflare_list item ownership.
resources.evidence: The Cloudflare Lists generator enumerates /accounts/{account_id}/rules/lists/{list_id}/items and emits those entries under cloudflare_list items.# attributes. A separate cloudflare_list_item importer would create a second Terraform ownership path for the same list entries.

resources.reason: Logpush ownership challenge is a verification action rather than durable configuration.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource requires a sensitive destination_conf and returns challenge filename, message, and valid status. Terraformer already imports durable cloudflare_logpush_job resources, so challenge generation should not be part of broad import.

resources.reason: Magic Network Monitoring account configuration does not expose a provider import contract.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 implements read/create/update/delete for cloudflare_magic_network_monitoring_configuration but does not implement ResourceWithImportState or document terraform import. The resource owns account-level router and WARP-device monitoring configuration, so Terraformer cannot safely seed it through provider import.

resources.reason: Magic Transit connectors can require generated or write-only license material that broad import cannot reconstruct.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 marks license_key as Sensitive and documents it is only returned on creation, while device.provision_license is a write-only creation field. Provider import verification ignores these fields, so Terraformer cannot emit a complete connector resource from discovery alone.

resources.reason: Organization resources model Cloudflare organization identity lifecycle rather than zone or account settings inventory.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider can import by organization_id and owns organization identity/profile fields. Broad import in the settings lane would claim Cloudflare account hierarchy state that should remain explicitly user-authored.

resources.reason: Organization profile resources do not expose a Terraform provider import path.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 documents that cloudflare_organization_profile does not currently support terraform import. Terraformer cannot reliably discover and refresh this profile as generated Terraform configuration.

resources.reason: Pipeline sink config can contain write-only authentication material that broad import cannot reconstruct.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 marks config.credentials.secret_access_key and config.token as Sensitive/no_refresh, and provider import verification ignores config. Terraformer cannot safely emit credential-bearing sink configuration from discovery alone.

resources.reason: R2 Sippy replication requires source or destination credential material that cannot be safely reconstructed.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_r2_bucket_sippy as not supporting terraform import and marks source/destination secret_access_key plus GCS private_key fields as Sensitive/no_refresh.

resources.reason: R2 managed domain provider semantics do not expose a usable broad import/read path.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_r2_managed_domain as not supporting terraform import, and the generated resource has empty Read and Delete methods while warning that the managed domain cannot be destroyed from Terraform.

resources.reason: Registrar domain resources do not expose a Terraform provider import path and are tied to registrar/billing lifecycle.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 documents that cloudflare_registrar_domain does not currently support terraform import. Domain registration state is not a zone/account settings singleton and should not be synthesized by broad settings discovery.

resources.reason: Schema validation operation settings do not currently have a Terraform provider import contract.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_schema_validation_operation_settings as not supporting terraform import. Although the API lists per-operation mitigation settings, Terraformer cannot seed this resource through provider import until the provider exposes ResourceWithImportState.

resources.reason: Schema validation zone settings do not currently have a Terraform provider import contract.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_schema_validation_settings as not supporting terraform import. The resource owns zone-level mitigation defaults and overrides, so Terraformer should not emit it until provider import support and default-handling are explicit.

resources.reason: Snippet resources require source files and the Terraform provider does not currently support import.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider docs require snippet files, metadata, and snippet_name, and explicitly state that cloudflare_snippet does not currently support terraform import.

resources.reason: SSO connector resources include identity-provider verification workflow state that broad settings import should not claim.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider imports by account_id/sso_connector_id and exposes begin_verification plus DNS verification code/status. Importing discovered connectors would mix durable SSO configuration with Cloudflare-generated verification lifecycle state outside this settings lane.

resources.reason: Stream download resources represent generated media output state instead of durable infrastructure configuration.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource requires a media identifier and reads download status, percent_complete, and generated URLs for audio/default downloads. Broad import would capture runtime generation progress rather than desired configuration.

resources.reason: Stream signing keys expose generated private key material that broad import should not materialize.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 supports account-scoped import, but the jwk and pem attributes are Sensitive/no_refresh generated signing keys. Broad import would capture generated secret key material rather than reconstruct user-authored configuration.

resources.reason: Stream live input resources expose generated streaming credentials and the provider does not support import.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_stream_live_input as not supporting terraform import and marks generated RTMPS/SRT/WebRTC URLs, stream keys, and passphrases as Sensitive.

resources.reason: Stream webhook resources expose generated verification secret material and the provider does not support import.
resources.evidence: Terraform provider v5.19.1 documents cloudflare_stream_webhook as not supporting terraform import and marks the generated webhook secret as Sensitive.

resources.reason: User resources do not expose a Terraform provider import path and represent identity/account membership state.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 documents that cloudflare_user does not currently support terraform import. The resource reads user identity, organizations, roles, and two-factor status, which are not durable zone/account settings for broad import.

resources.reason: User groups are account access-management objects, not zone/account settings, and include policy ownership that needs explicit user intent.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider imports by account_id/user_group_id and owns group policies with permission and resource groups. Broad settings import would claim IAM policy configuration that should remain in a dedicated access-management lane.

resources.reason: User group membership manages account identity relationships rather than durable zone/account settings.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider imports by account_id/user_group_id and owns the members list for existing account members. Broad import could unexpectedly make Terraformer authoritative for user membership lifecycle.

resources.reason: Scanner credentials require write-only secret material.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider schema requires value as a Sensitive credential value and documents it as write-only and never returned in responses. Terraformer cannot discover the required value for generated HCL.

resources.reason: Worker version resources couple runtime version state to code modules, assets, and bindings that broad discovery cannot safely reconstruct.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource includes modules, main_module, assets, bindings, migrations, and sensitive asset or binding values. Terraformer currently imports Workers routes, custom domains, cron triggers, and dispatch namespaces, but not script body or version ownership.

resources.reason: Workers deployment resources model active rollout/version state and can conflict with separate script or version ownership.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource requires script_name, rollout strategy, and version percentage mappings. Issue #335 calls out Workers/Scripts/Snippets/Workflows body and version resources as needing careful handling before import.

resources.reason: Workers KV entries are high-cardinality content records, not stable provider-level infrastructure inventory.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource requires namespace_id, key_name, and value for each KV pair, with values up to 25 MiB. Terraformer currently imports cloudflare_workers_kv_namespace, leaving individual KV content records out of broad storage import.

resources.reason: Worker scripts can include source code, assets, modules, and sensitive binding material that should not be reconstructed blindly.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource accepts content/content_file, assets, bindings, migrations, and sensitive asset or binding fields. Terraformer currently imports Workers routes, custom domains, cron triggers, and dispatch namespaces, but not script body ownership.

resources.reason: Workflow resources are tied to Worker script/class ownership and runtime execution state.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource requires workflow_name, class_name, and script_name, while read state includes runtime instance counters, deletion state, trigger timestamps, and version_id. Import should be handled together with Workers script/version ownership decisions.

resources.reason: MCP server resources can require write-only authentication credentials and expose sync/runtime state.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 marks auth_credentials as Sensitive and models sync status, tool, prompt, error, and last_synced fields. Terraformer cannot recover the credential value or distinguish desired tool/prompt updates from runtime sync state during broad import.

resources.reason: Device posture integrations require provider-specific credential/configuration material that needs a dedicated reconstruction design.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 requires config, interval, name, and type. Integration config varies by third-party posture provider and can contain connector credentials or tenant-specific setup values that should not be inferred from broad account discovery.

resources.reason: Custom DLP entries need body/pattern reconstruction and should be handled with the broader DLP ownership model.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 requires enabled, name, and pattern. DLP entry APIs are shared with predefined and integration-backed entries, so broad import needs a dedicated design for entry type separation, profile attachments, and sensitive matching body ownership.

resources.reason: Custom DLP profiles own entry sets, data classes, tags, sensitivity levels, and shared entries that need a profile/entry reconstruction design.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 exposes entries, shared_entries, data_classes, data_tags, sensitivity_levels, context_awareness, OCR, and AI context settings. Importing profiles independently from entries can produce incomplete or conflicting DLP ownership.

resources.reason: DLP datasets are high-cardinality content/body resources with generated upload/version state.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 models dataset contents, columns, uploads, status, version, max_cells, and num_cells. Broad import should not reconstruct dataset bodies or generated upload lifecycle state without a dedicated design.

resources.reason: Generic DLP entry imports would duplicate or obscure custom, predefined, and integration entry ownership.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 requires enabled, name, and pattern for the generic entry resource while separate custom, predefined, and integration entry resources use the same DLP entry API family. A DLP sweep needs type-aware ownership before Terraformer can emit usable HCL.

resources.reason: DLP integration entries depend on third-party integration state and profile attachment ownership.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 requires entry_id and enabled, and reads profiles, upload_status, variant, word_list, and integration-derived metadata. Broad import needs integration-aware handling instead of emitting entries independently.

resources.reason: Predefined DLP entries are Cloudflare catalog entries whose enabled state overlaps with predefined profile configuration.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 requires entry_id and enabled while predefined profile resources also own enabled entry sets. Broad import would need to avoid duplicate ownership between entry and profile resources.

resources.reason: Predefined DLP profiles mix Cloudflare catalog state with account-specific enabled entry ownership.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 requires profile_id and can own enabled_entries while also reading catalog entries, OCR, AI context, and sensitivity settings. Import should be handled with a DLP profile/entry ownership design.

resources.reason: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 documents Zero Trust organization settings as not supporting terraform import.
resources.evidence: The Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 resource documentation states that cloudflare_zero_trust_organization does not currently support terraform import. Terraformer should not emit this broad Access organization singleton until the provider import contract is documented as supported.

resources.reason: Risk scoring integrations are third-party tenant integrations with lifecycle state that should not be inferred from broad discovery.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 requires integration_type and tenant_url, while read state includes active, account_tag, reference_id, well_known_url, and creation state. Import needs integration-specific handling to avoid emitting tenant-managed setup state as Terraform-owned configuration.

resources.reason: WARP connector resources require a sensitive tunnel secret and expose runtime connector status.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 marks tunnel_secret as Sensitive while read state includes connections, active/inactive timestamps, HA state, metadata, status, and tunnel type. Terraformer cannot recover the secret or safely own connector runtime state from discovery.

resources.reason: Zone DNS settings remain deferred because the provider has no import path and API reads return effective zone settings without explicit ownership markers.
resources.evidence: Cloudflare provider v5.19.1 documents that cloudflare_zone_dns_settings does not support terraform import. Its read path returns the full zone DNS settings object, including nameserver, SOA, multi-provider, secondary DNS, and internal DNS options, but does not distinguish inherited/default values from zone-authored configuration.

resources.reason: Zone subscription state is Cloudflare billing lifecycle state and should not be imported as routine infrastructure configuration.
resources.evidence: The Terraform provider resource requires Billing Read/Write permissions and models frequency, rate_plan, price, current billing periods, subscription state, and externally_managed rate-plan metadata.
